AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

1:1 consultation and updates on business progress. Laura Gallon has shifted from selling jewelry to focusing on candles after customers found her jewelry prices too high. She creates both decorative candles (including souvenir/gift candles for events like baby showers) and aromatic candles. 


Her main challenges include the high cost of quality supplies (especially soy wax), limited Eng

Pictures sent to Nancy, Marketing at Lead Center for success story.

 

From: Marie Elliott

Sent: Wednesday, May 14, 2025 1:05 PM

To: Nancy Kooienga <kooiengn@gvsu.edu>

Subject: RE: New submission from Client Story Submission


Hi Nancy,

called Larry while on the road. he's been fishing commercially as a tribal member for years. spent a few years on Beaver Island and ran the fresh fish market. served fresh fried fish at festivals and events. lives in Honor now, fishes out of Leland and would like to purchase a food trailer to fry fish again. talked about Cherry Festival and other music/event festivals. would like to form LLC. in process of discussions with gentleman who is selling a food trailer for $20,000. Larry says he needs help with marketing, but first we need to see if the food trailer is actually a viable option. he can start fishing anytime he needs/wants. currently he sells caviar to out of state buyers. min order 1000 lbs. I will send Larry LLC formation info and 20F/VN SBSH grant info for him to look over before we meet next week.

Media.Other: I found your website on a search engine

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Phone call with Bill today. Bill provided an update on his video. He did a few runs of his video but did not like them indoors. He is paying a Former Tour Pro to do the video and they are waiting to do it outdoors. He is thinking in the next month they will complete the video.


I asked Bill for feedback on his experience with co-consulting with the Growth Team. His response was I laid the groundwork but Dale provided the expertise on the video content and delivery. He shared Dale closed the gap on the video with him and gave him the confidence to move forward with his business idea. He stated, without Dale their is a high possibility the video would not have happened. His enthusiasm is high right now and he is moving forward.


We discussed that his file is "not yet in business" but he shared he is paying a significant amount for the Former Tour Pro to do the video. I recommended he formalize his business and asked if he will be operating und

He has been working on delegating responsibilities to his brother Philip and other staff members, which is helping him focu

Introduction and Background

  • drua@sienaheights.edu mentioned that they were on their way to a work conference but could still talk.
  • drua@sienaheights.edu explained that they were developing a project and needed to discuss it with Nancy as per Gary's instructions.

Key Points

  • Nancy explains her role in the SBDC tech team and the services they offer, including the Business Accelerator Fund, which provides grants for services like design, marketing, legal, and software testing.

Summary: The client wrote that a school district recently asked them for a single source letter, and he was reaching out to see if I had experience with this — or know what they should be aware of when preparing one.


In their case, it seems reasonable, especially since their interactive videos are proprietary, adapt to student ability and learning pace, and they own the copyright. That said, he is still getting up to speed on what’s typically expected or required in these letters.


Also he wanted to know if there’s any connection they can make to their federal SBIR grant funding here. He recalls reading that SBIR-funded products can be listed as "single source" on the GSA, but he hasn’t been able to track down the specifics.


Action: I responded that I have only been asked about this in relation to DoD Phase III projects and I always refer the client to their local APEX accelerator for help. I am not sure if there could be

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: AACORN is a nonprofit day program for autistic and neuro challenged clients. They are planning to start a residential program and build a community of homes and operate it as a for profit housing project. They have been working with many developers and contractors and this meeting was to review the business plan and financial projections to determine the funding needed. This meeting also introduced Britt Darden, an SBDC client who is starting a consulting business to assist indigenous farmers and landowners on how to use the funding available to purchase land and develop it into residential communities and operating farms. AACORN and Britt will be working together. The AACORN case will be closed because they are going to stay a nonprofit only and if they go the for profit route for the new program, they will contact the office.

Scheduled welcome email, respond to team welcome, update shared docs, review calendar availability, adjust as needed



TRose Malone

The client is a licensed social worker currently employed within a healthcare organization and is exploring the transition to starting a private practice. During our meeting, we discussed the following key areas:


  • Clarifying the client’s niche and specific services to be offered
  • Selecting an appropriate legal business structure
  • Identifying and obtaining the necessary insurance coverage
  • Defining fee structures and available payment options
  • Identifying the ideal customer or target client base
  • Developing a marketing strategy for the practice
  • Estimating startup costs and ongoing monthly expenses
  • Recommending tools to track income and expenses for tax purposes
  • Outlining and drafting a comprehensive business plan

ClientIntake.BusinessType: Real Estate and Rental and Leasing

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
PROBLEM

Fight stagnation and mental inertia


DISCUSSION

Revenue fuels growth - and innovation causes exponential scalability. I want to help you fortify your sales strategy for the 3rd and 4th quarter of 2025.

We are almost ALWAYS evolving through cycles of LIFE - let alone BUSINESS. I reached out today with a subtle nudge to affirm client

*file upload


Copy of Cash Receipt Master Copy.xlsx

*file upload


Copy of Master COPY Invoice Template.xlsx

Client indicated that she had 18 new items posted in her online store. She also indicated that she finalize set up of her payment system for her website. The idea is to use her website as her e-commerce platform.


Her next event will be Memorial Day weekend. She still expresses some apprehension for these type of events, but after talking through them, she realizes that although she doesn’t make a lot of sales, She is generating a lot of interest in her product offering. She also recognizes the importance of this is a marketing channel for her product.


She also identified in our meeting this week that her friend Carla, who previously worked for Steve Madden shoes, as a potential partner and investor in her business.

We are almost ALWAYS evolving through cycles of LIFE - let alone BUSINESS. I reached out today with a subtle nudge to affirm client remains ever thinking, dreaming - and DOING.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Margaret Warren (no longer Bradford) has come back as an active client with her business of Princess Poo high end porta potties. Todays session was a review of her business plan which is handwritten and will be typed up and made more legible and complete. Her financials are also hand written at the end of the business plan. These need to be entered into a spreadsheet so that they are easier to comprehend and review. She knows the porta potty business very well and has managed a water treatment plant. She has been working on paying down her debt and increasing her credit score after a divorce and is now feeling much more confident in getting a business loan. She would like to start with Advia CU or an SBA loan if possible. She will be setting up her LLC, preparing the business plan to be ready to meet a lender. I have shared this info with Bonnie/ RD to see if we should transition her to another consultant now. I suggested Nate help her with formatting the financial info a

Media.Other: null - Online search for Michigan business gov

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Suzanne was able to have a conversation with Chris Chamberlain of real Traverse Bay fair Company. Chris indicated that he has margin to work with in regard to using pay tripper as a booking agent for the ferry company. Suzanne will schedule a future meeting with Chris to further the conversation.


We talked about strategies and tactics for attracting exhibitors within the physical space. She feels that she needs approximately 6 to 8 exhibitors at a fee of $300 to $350 per month.


She indicated that she is in the process of putting together a task force of local storytellers to include Brian Wagner, and Carlin Smith from Petoskey.


Part of this will involve inventorying cultural assets in the area.


She also talked to great length about the need for a preservationist role in the community. she feels that this ties directly into preserving cultural elements native to our region.


Additionally, we discusse

ClientIntake.CompanyName: Royal Oath Inc. DBA Royal Oath Insurance Group

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Email to Dan Brophy


I met with a client today who has a few insurance businesses and a separate health and wellness company. Requested coaching and financing. Revenue is $800k (though he said that it may be a million by end of year.) Has 12 employees. 


For the coaching, I think the Focus Five is what he is looking for. 


As for financing. I get the sense that he is not being totally forthcoming. Credit score is 630 and profit is $25k (but this is basically his salary). He banks at Huntington, but says they are hard to deal with. Wants $150k to consolidate debt and hire people. Has not finished 2024 taxes, but will send 3 years when they are done. 


Not sure how much leeway your have these days, but I definitely think he would benefit from a structured program. If not us, then maybe 10,000 (I assume it is still taking people). However, I did want to point out the feeling I get around the financing part. The numbers are

  • Favorite Places: this is showing where people who went to Scarefest also go to at a minimum twice in 2024. I filtered out a lot of non-retail categories so you could see in this top ten list places people shop, eat, etc.

CRITICAL ISSUE: After he had a shortfall in funding, Dalton had backed off from purchasing this business. Last week I learned about a MEDC Collateral program that might make the difference in him being able to buy. Looked at the Quality Wood Products website to see if it was still operating under the same owner, and it appears to be; then sent Dalton the following email:

Hello Dalton, 


I wanted to check with you again and see if this was still available and something you would be interested in buying. Last week in a meeting I found out about a program through MEDC that might be helpful for you to purchase the business. Since cash flow was the main reason you were unable to purchase, this would help alleviate that issue and keep the business running. I just looked at their website, and its still up, but nothing is on there about it being for sale. Keeping this business going and expanding with your plans would be great for our area. 


If you ar

Email/response


It sounds like you have found a good niche on Thumbtack. I am concerned if you change from individual to company, you may lose the testimonials. 


In terms of industry, I would look at competitors who are companies. How much are they paying their tutors? Do they pay travel costs and how is that determined? Is the $5 referral for every session? 


Have you set up a company yet? Because you will have employees, you may have legal liability and may want to start an LLC. We have a 24/7 workshop:  Forming your LLC or DBA: https://lms.michigansbdc.org/course?courseid=forming-your-llc-or-dba


Here are some other points for you

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Emails with Hunter and Cassie detailing the start of their business and the impact collected. They took ownership on April 28, and the business was purchased for $80,000, which was financed by a downpayment from them and seller financing. Traditional lender was not an option since the tax returns did not match the internal financials the clients were provided. Hunter also shared they've invested an extra $4,500 for working capital. They hired the 5 employees the previous owner had on her staff, so along with Hunter and Cassie, there are 7 new jobs. Also explained to Hunter how they can continue to work with the SBDC going forward.


Impact:

  • 1 Startup
  • 7 jobs created
  • $84,500 economic impact
    • $70,000 in seller financing
    • $10,000 in owner cash injection for down payment
    • $4500 in owner cash injection for working capital

Justin was tied up at the winery and not available to meet today. Meeting was with Justin‘s partner, Pearce.


It was noted that they had been working on an AI assisted business plan, which does not include production of wine at this stage. This new strategy will outsource production to a local partner. This is intended to generate interest in their product while crop production comes online.


They identified that they will use local beaver Island, real estate agent, Ed Wojan as their broker and buyers agent. In preliminary discussions with Ed client noted that they would be offering the asking price for the initial 10 acres and a discounted price for the 40 acres covered under land contract.


The business plan is being revised and should be forwarded to consultant within the next couple days.

Ruthie is a Medical Assistant who has been certified as a nail technician. She is interested in changing careers for financial reasons, but hasn't found any clients in spite of trying for over a year. She's stated that people are reluctant to switch their nail techs, which is one of the reasons that she wants to target seniors and people with disabilities. She wants to purchase and refit a small bus or van as a mobile salon and bring it to homes and facilities.


I encouraged her to research what that might cost (including plumbing, ventilation, decoration, etc.) to purchase as well as operate. I also encouraged her to estimate her direct costs to serve a client, how long it might take and how much she could clear on each to be able to estimate what she'll be able to take home.


She has an interview with a nearby salon tonight which I was very supportive of. I told her she needs to start working with clients asap. She is licensed

Client is very excited about completion of her very first podcast.


Client noted that she now has 5000 Facebook followers and continues to attract more. She’s excited to see how many additional followers she will get from her podcast.


Her podcast are located on the YouTube channel “the coach, Kim Larson“. We discussed goals for the number of views that she has for her podcast so that she can start to measure progress. She noted that her goal for her first podcast was 10 views, and that her second podcast would be 20 views. We discussed the importance of having measurable objectives for her to gauge progress and celebrate the winds that come with those.


She did know that there has been no change in active clients.


She indicated that there are more podcast to follow, and is excited to keep those moving along.

Summary: The client wrote that they have reached out to the NIH to connect with a Program Manager a few times since they last met but have not heard back as of yet. Given the current state of cuts in research personnel and funding, they wanted to know if I think they should still move forward and aim for a Sept 2025 submission. They wanted to know if I have any insights into how the cuts may have impacted SBIR/STTR funding.


Action: I responded that my personal opinion is that they should keep going and try to submit. To the best of my knowledge, NIH is still accepting, reviewing, and awarding SBIR/STTR grants. The things that are changing most is who is still there and able to respond. If they are unable to get a response to email, I would try the phone number (to see if it gets answered and/or routed). Unless there are cuts in the extramural research budget, there cannot (by congressional law) be cuts to the SBIR/STTR programs because they are a mandated percentag

Summary: The client asked if I could send them the media release agreement to review it with the team, but I don't want to create another salesforce entry and then not finish the application. He also wanted to know how the tax liability works with SBIR funds. If they pay it all out in salaries/subcontractors and equipment, they wanted to know if they have a tax liability at the end of the year.


Action: I responed attached is the MEDC release agreement. I believe this is what they are looking for but if not, let me know and I will see if I can figure it out. Tax liability is tricky. Before 2021 what they say is the case. However, in 2021 they slipped in an awful bill that made this not so. The problem: Section 174. Previously, any money spent on research was fully tax deductible in the year it was spent. So if a company spent $100 on research they could deduct that on their taxes as a business expense. Now, the company has to amortize the expenses over 5 years.

I reviewed Mr. Derrel Watt's business plan. After reviewing his business plan I read through your narrative and it looked good. My concern is that the SBA 7(a) loan is primarily designed for existing businesses rather than startups because it requires businesses to demonstrate creditworthiness and a reasonable ability to repay the loan. Since startups typically lack an established financial history, they may struggle to meet these requirements.

However, startups aren’t completely excluded—if they can show strong financial projections, collateral, and a solid business plan, they may still qualify. Alternatively, the SBA offers microloans and 504 loans, which may be better suited for new businesses looking for funding. With this i

Quarterly Board of Advisors Meeting

See file attached

CRITICAL PROBLEMS: Nick has really gone at this plan with zeal and had a large portion of the written section completed and input some of the financial data. However, the numbers are very skewed, with very high personnel costs (35% of gross revenue?) and this is based on what the current owners gave him for financial history with last year's tax return. He had the total annual revenue by category, but not a monthly breakdown; and we had to find a way to do monthly projections based on anecdotal reports (most business October -March, extremely slow and closed down in July for half a month; and no details of staffing for each part of the business (lounge, bowling, food). Current owners and their parents did a lot of cash business that wasn't recorded, so that skewed the personnel costs higher.


2 hours prep before session reading through and making notes in Live Plan.


RECOMMENDATIONS:

  1. Breakdown % of revenue by category. Take total

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Call with Daniel. He is found a new application for his vacuum drying technology: drying wet grains from beer breweries. This can be used in combinations with "charging" biochar from the water that is removed. He hadn't yet worked out the overall process economics, so I helped him go through this calculation. We identified missing costs he hadn't considered. I recommended two alternative business models for this application and we discussed their pros and cons. Next steps will be to validated some of the open ended assumptions he made in process economics and creating a financial forecast once he selects the business model that he would like to pursue. Additional prep time for researching biochar and the validity of his value prop.

Summary: The client wrote thanking me for taking the time to talk with him. It seems like what they were hoping to do regarding the SBIR will not be possible for logistical reasons; they will follow another plan, so there is no need to meet on short notice. That said, they did start a business and are interested in pursuing this venture. They are now thinking about how to set themselves up to compete for such opportunities next time. They would love to talk to them about this in the future.


Action: I responded that I am sorry to hear, but I understand, especially given the short turnaround. I am happy to set up another meeting to discuss, but first they will need to fill out the form that I sent.


Plan: They will let me know if/when they would like to look at a meeting and they can set something up.

Summary: The client wrote that he recently wrote a Project Overview in his efforts to raise capital from a local microlender. In the proposal he had to write a Methodology, which he believes may be similar to what is needed for the technical portion of the NSF proposal. If awarded, the loan will be allocated to initiate the development of the MVP, as well as commission his engineer (PI) to assist with the proposal before submitting it to the NSF to make his company a stronger candidate (He does not want to ask the engineer for free work or compensate beneath his normal rate). He wanted the grant initially but this may be his only option. However, if the Methodology from the Project Proposal is sufficient, he could possibly submit the Methodology portion with the NSF application, with minor revisions. B: This is a new project for client and is still in the ideation stage. Client contacted me last week to say that he has a new idea for a business. Basically, he want's to create an online validation platform for crypto currencies. The problem that he is addressing is that of fraud. There are a plethora of scams in the crypto world, especially as they relate to Alt coins. Client want's to create a platform whereby crypto buyers are able to validate the safety and accuracy of claims regarding a particular crypto currency token. He claims that there are no such services available.


A: Client is looking for help to develop the concept and fund it. There are a number of clear roadblocks: (i) some crypto exchanges thru there listing process do validate many tokens ( tho' a very small % of the number out there), (ii) client is a one man shop, (iii) demand for such a service is unclear, and (iv) the funding environment in the crypto area is challenging.

Good morning Charlie,

I have decided not to pursue the opportunity at this time.


Regards,


Terry

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
I met with Stacy to discuss her application materials for the GLBBH grant.

We reviewed the executive summary that she began working on. She started by pulling text from her business plan and adding it to corresponding sections of the executive summary template. Some of the information that she has is good content, but it needs some polishing to help it flow.

We discussed each section at a high level, and I helped her find more information in her plan to use as a starting point. She also had some questions about the financial questions. I advised we could use her business plan's figures for the requested projection piece, and her financial assumptions will help make sense of the projections as well.

By the time we finished our meeting, most of the sections had at least some content to work with. We will meet again tomorrow to polish this piece and discuss the supplemental required documents.

Yugo and Julia are refining their financial model and projections but are grappling with aligning spending with industry benchmarks, accurately projecting subscriber growth, and calculating realistic fundraising needs. Their current model includes sharp jumps in subscriber numbers, underestimates marketing spend, and lacks adjustments for pre-launch costs tied to a planned revenue increase in early 2027. They’re also evaluating whether current headcount and salary allocations for account managers and G&A roles are appropriate as the business scales. Yugo is preparing updated financials for Spark and needs to ensure the model reflects a sustainable, scalable growth trajectory that investors will find credible.


I helped them identify areas where projections could be more realistic—such as smoothing subscriber growth with a 5% monthly increase and tying headcount and costs more closely to industry-standard SaaS benchmarks. I walked them through cost allocation stra

A.V. is developing a prototype AI system that enables voice-controlled operation of both physical and virtual robots. While he’s demonstrated its simplicity through presentations to students, he faces significant challenges moving forward without clear customer insights from his intended market—large manufacturing companies. He’s competing with established players like Siemens and PTC, which adds complexity to selling his product. A.V. is also navigating uncertainty around product positioning and the most effective way to conduct outreach and gather meaningful customer feedback, all while being pulled toward secondary markets like hobbyists for quicker traction.


I recommended that A.V. narrow his focus and commit to the primary market—manufacturing companies—rather than spreading efforts across multiple segments. I emphasized the importance of structured customer discovery, guiding him to begin conversations not by pitching features but by deeply understanding curre

Mon 5/19/2025 12:01 PM

Hi Kara,

I see how you've approached it and this is just fine. Well done!


As for your office expenses and more - all of it could be expensed back to the business. There's also a way to calculate the "office". $50/mo seems low to me. 


Example - what % is the total square footage of the office portion to the rest of your home? If it's 10% of the total, you can expense 10% of your real estate taxes, utilities, and mortgage (if you have one). You can also take your phone and internet as well as an expense - check with your accountant, please. 


Your accountant may want to make cert

I met with Tom at his shop to discuss the Executive Summary section of the GLBBH grant application packet. He had expressed some frustration with the executive summary template, noting that some of the information is not easy to come up with. I advised that the template was originally meant for a tech start up, and he was not obligated to answer every question if it did not pertain to his business.

We also discussed the possibility of using ChatGPT to help with the flow of the narrative if he was feeling stuck. Tom's wife opposes AI for religious reasons, though he may try it from time to time.

We also discussed how funds can be used. His main focus is on education for his staff, as he is hoping to hand the business over to one of his employees in five years.

He was going to make some revisions and send me a new copy of his executive summary to review. We agreed to meet again later in the week if needed for any final touches on his executive summary.

Hi Erica,

I hope you’re doing well! I wanted to thank you again for our meeting earlier this week. I apologize for the delay in following up—I've been catching up on emails and client work after being away for two weeks and just returned from our annual MI SBDC State Conference.

It was great learning more about your plans for Riri’s Logistics and Transportation! You’re off to a solid start with your LLC and EIN already in place. As we discussed, the next key step is building out your business plan so we can explore funding and marketing options. I'm glad you’re working with Advia, and we’ll continue talking through hiring plans and promotion strategies as your business grows.

Summary: The client sent an updated version of their NSF SBIR project pitch based on my previous comments, highlighting the innovations and challenges of the proposed work. They asked me to critique again.


Action: I have looked over what they sent me and they have a lot of great information here. However, it is almost too much information for a pitch and I am struggling where to suggest making cuts because it is in the wrong format.  To that end, I am sending the specific template for this document to help align it better with what the NSF expects in each section (along with length requirements) so that they can tailor it to what they will accept and then I can make suggestions based on that. Specifically: I can work with the first section which will ultimately become their Technical Innovation part of the pitch. They can just put that information into this part of the template and I will take a look at it in context with the rest of the pitch information. I imagin

Daniel is building Brava Games with a team of recent MSU graduates, and he’s navigating the financial and operational challenges of launching a new game studio. His main concerns include securing funding for legal expenses, developing accurate financial projections, estimating future game revenue, and maintaining team momentum after project completion. He’s also wrestling with how to fairly compensate a team working on a revenue-share basis while preparing for a game launch without much existing audience. Finding time and strategy for marketing—particularly via social media—and preparing for gaming conventions like Capital City Comic Con were additional challenges he’s working through.


I walked Daniel through how to use a financial forecasting spreadsheet, including how to set up assumptions, track actual expenses, and project team compensation with a blend of fixed salary and revenue sharing. We discussed marketing strategies focused on building community early thr

HI Diego, 


What happens if I have applied for my Business name and have now decided to wait a while longer to start up this adventure? Do I need to cancel or change anything? 


Thank you, 


Kelly

Hi Kelly,

Thank you for reaching out!

If you've reserved a business name with the Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs (LARA) but have decided to postpone starting your business, here's what you need to know:

  • Name Reservation Duration: A reserved business name in Michigan is held for 180 days. If you don't form your LLC within this period, the reservation will expire, and the name becomes available for others to reserve.
  • No Immediate Action Required: If you haven't filed the Articles of Organization to officially form your LLC, there's no need to cancel or change anything. Simply allowing the reservation to expire is sufficient.
  • Renewing the Reservation: Should you wish to extend the reservation, you can file another application and pay the associated fee to reserve the name for an additional 180 days.

If you have any further questions or need

I already paid for the name. Is there something I need to do? Thank you for the information above, but those are not the options for me. Do you have any other information?


Thank you!

CRITICAL PROBLEM: Michael had his two independent contractors attend so all three of them were clear on the tax and other implications of contractor vs employee.


RECOMMENDATIONS:

  1. Went through the IRS classification worksheet/rules.
  2. Explained that each of them would be responsible for paying their own taxes, but that they will also be able to claim business deductions for mileage, training, etc. One of them has a regular wage job, and wondered how to do that tax wise.
  3. Advised them to save 15% of what they earn from contract work in a savings account in year one. This will cover any tax liability they incur, and after they do their taxes next year they will pay in quarterly.
  4. After the session, I did a list of how to start a biz for them, what they need to keep track of, how contracts are setup, and more.

Re: Business Plan

Mariah Goos <mgoos@mtu.

Hi Kelly,

Thanks for the clarification.

If you’ve already filed and paid to form your LLC (not just reserved the name), there’s no immediate action required just because you’ve decided to delay starting the business.

Here’s what you should know:

  • Your LLC is officially formed and active with the State of Michigan.
  • There’s no requirement to start operating right away. Many people form their LLCs and take time to prepare before launching.
  • The only thing to keep in mind is the annual report. Each year, all Michigan LLCs must file an annual statement (and pay a $25 fee) with LARA by February 15 to remain in good standing, regardless of whether the business is actively operating.

If you’d prefer to shut it down for now, you could voluntarily dissolve the LLC by

She has been having trouble with technology, and doesn't seem to quite understand the prompts of the executive summary. Additionally, Kathy is facing a major cashflow issue. Her automated water teller is down, meaning that her customers are restricted to in-person purchases during business hour. The hit to her revenue is preventing her from repairing the machine or purchasing new inventory. She is ready to throw in the towel on the business altogether at this point.

We worked through the questions she had on the executive summary so far, and I made some suggestions to improve her narrative. We also talked about how she could use the funds. Using previous quotes, we tallied up around $12,000 in inventory purchases she could make with grant dollars, if awarded.

SBA 7a Loan Conversation

I talked with Pam about the SBA 7a Loan. I explained to her what it is, what the purpose of it is as well as what is needed in terms of a bank loan.


It looks like her project would cost a total of around 750,000 meaning that she she would need between 75,000 - 140,000 as down payment for the business purchase.

She has around 30,000 prepared.


Pam still would like to pursue the process and begin looking for ways to finance so we are going to put together cash flow information.


Pam does not have any income coming in at this time and I explained to her that, that is a red flag to the bank, they need to see that you can make your payments. I sent her the cash flow template, as well as a guide for SBA 7a loans

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Had a few texts and a call with Deborah. She is having problems with her new hire. He says he will get something done but doesn't deliver. He also does not do a thorough job and doesn't meet timing expectations for customers.


This is the third manager who Deborah has had these issues with....clearly it could be three bad hires or, more likely, it is Deborah not being clear with expectations and duties and responsibilities.


Deb needs to make some positive moves. She probably needs to sell the business while it is still worth something, especially now that she fully owns the buidling and her estate issues have been sorted.

Jennifer and I met to discuss the GLBBH grant. She had gone through an intake meeting with Elizabeth of CMURC and myself in the fall of 2024, but decided not to pursue the grant during cycle 1. She was considering writing an application for the final cycle, but had some questions.

We discussed the application materials. Jennifer was concerned about being able to get it done in three days. Knowing Jennifer's high level of writing skills, that wasn't necessarily a concern I shared, but I did warn her that it takes some time to sit down and focus to get a draft completed.

Jennifer also had questions about what the funds could be used for. She was interested in using the funds towards inventory. I advised that this would be an allowable use of funds. However, the issue is that the Reids purchase their inventory as they find it. There is no certain way for them to document what exactly they will purchase, particularly because so much of it is bought secondhand.

LLC for BBQ business has been filed. Tammy and Brian are in a hurry but thankfully they didn't give away BBQ on the corner of their property for donations. They are looking at applying to Michigan Rehabilitation Serves to help with the costs of start up and licensing.


We'll start on the business plan in our next meeting as MRS will require it.


They are waiting for the packet of forms from the health department. I agreed to help if they needed help.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Grant is launching a space sensor startup based on patented technology he developed during his PhD and is now facing the challenge of turning that invention into a viable business. He’s navigating early-stage decisions about business models, IP protection, and funding, while also preparing applications for competitive grant programs like NASA SBIR and the Tech Leap Prize. He’s concerned about how to approach large players like SpaceX without risking exploitation, how to manage licensing with the University of Michigan, and how to prioritize building the right team and partnerships before revenue is flowing.


I helped Grant think through different business models—hardware sales, software subscriptions, licensing, and consulting—while we explored how he might create value for both small and large customers. We discussed strategies for protecting his IP, including examining patent claims for potential workarounds and using an option-to-license structure before fully com

Jeff,

 

Attached to this email is the Internal Business Valuation for Wise H& C. The estimated value is $1,200,000.

Summary: Robin Kinnie reached out with a client who is very interested in applying for the SBIR program and is looking to connect with an advisor or consultant who can help guide them through the process.


Action: I responded that it is a pleasure to meet them through Robin. I will start by giving they a little of my background so that they can see if there is something that I can assist them with. I am a high-energy physicist who had a small business in Virginia before coming to Michigan. The R&D of their particle accelerator research company was funded by the SBIR and STTR programs and they had over 30 awards (phase I and II) in 8 of the federal agencies before selling off the business in the early 2000s. Instead of forming another business, I came to the SBDC to help others utilize the SBIR/STTR and other federal grant and contract programs.   I work in 5 general areas of the SBIR/STTR programs: SBIR/STTR program description ·        Basic rules of the SBIR/S

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: closing at this time. no response after previous outreach

Jessica set up a call to discuss her new policies, which she put on her website for any new or existing clients to see.

We went through each policy to make sure that they made sense, as well as check for any typos. From what I could see, all of the policies were both reasonable and were easy to understand. I don't think that she will have any issues from new clients, but some returning clients may be hard to gain approval.

We discussed in depth one of her clients, who is a stay at home mom who often cancels on Jessica, and the house usually takes over an hour more than estimated to clean to Jessica's standards. However, the client tends not to care if Jessica isn't able to get to something due to time restraints. To this end, Jessica is considering making special agreements which list out what services were completed, so that the customer cannot come back and complain if something not listed wasn't completed. I think this could be helpful. Jessica will

Call with Dustin. He was working (good) but he's looking for work with the union to keep money coming in. He said the bank wasn't great and have not told him much. I suggested he set up chat with the banker to see about a loan. The Petoskey brand of Huntington has not gotten back to him. An appointment and going in there will be different.


He wants help with the business plan financials and more. He feels alone. I am going to go to his office (home) and build a LivePlan with him on June 3.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Client has moved to Michigan from Florida and is starting her business here. She has not properly closed her Flori8a business or notified the State or the IRS. She needs to also register her business in MI. and get an EIN and business bank account. She has found a Holistic Dr. office in Troy to rent out a space for about 10 hrs a month and work out to that facility. Needs to find another simiar arrangement, perhaps with a Jewish Family services group.

RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

Working on finalising website and social media. Looking for ways to connect and engage different wellness groups (chiropracter, yoga, sport medicine, doulas, women's health to get a

Met with client to review market research options.


First, we had to review his information in the system as he had previously registered. I was able to work with him to update his information and he was able to give me the information he is looking for on market research.


We set up a meeting in two weeks and I will start the process for market research.

1:1 business planning session, prep, BARN notes




B - Reviewed client's business plan and addressed concerns and areas that need more development. Settled on tagline "Coffee. Books. Inclusive by Design." which emcompasses all of their value propostions. The business is focused on selling books and providing great coffee to local clients, and importantly, the books and target market are for and by marginalized, BICOP, queer and other communities that may not get as much attention. Bookstores tend to carry all of the same books, to the exclusion of other authors who are writing for a difference audience. Virtual book clubs as well. Feature Michigan authors.


A - Good idea

I met with Deanna and Aiyanna one more time to review the grant documents. Their narrative and supplemental materials are really compelling this round, particularly with the new partnerships and the training and experience Robby has gained in the last six months.

Once the final review was completed, I stood by as Deanna submitted the documents. We had to double-check a couple of times due to duplicate materials, but all of the most up-to-date pieces were submitted. From there, I said that I would be in touch as needed - decisions are released on June 13th, so we have about three weeks before we know for sure what will happen.

Met with David and Ryan (president of Leland Furniture, parent company of AutoExec) at their location in SW Grand Rapids.

  • Didn't get exact renenue numbers, but they are close to $500,000 YTD, with expected totals for the year to be $1,500,000. They feel they are profitable at over $2,000,000.
  • We discussed their B2C and B2B customers.
    • B2B
      • This is the main focus for David right now. They had one supplier drop them due to fulfillment issues when they were considering closing down. David claims he has lots of contacts within municipalities, utilities, and the outfitters who build the trucks and incorporate their product. Trade shows and following up on leads is the main activity David can be focusing on.
      • Clarified with Ryan agreeing that this is top priority. We discussed the idea of a standalone B2B website focusing on ROI, safety, and liability reduction. Ended up with idea of adding link to current website.
      • Is attending a municipa

After seeing Jurnee's last email/text, I reached out to her via phone once I was out of my meeting. She was concerned about having time to make the edits in time for the submission deadline. I reassured her that the vast majority of her information is already there in her executive summary! However, it may just need to be a little more specific in a few areas. I also encouraged her to at least use the Use of Funds and Proposal templates for her application. Those are helpful to give the reviewers some uniformity, and the Use of Funds is technically the legal agreement used to obligate the awardees to use funds as promised.

After we spoke, Jurnee seemed a little more sure of her abilities, and said that she would reach out if she had any other questions.

Summary:


Initial Discussion on the $5,000 App Development

  • collabgoceo@gmail.com apologized for losing patience regarding the $5,000 app development, expressing frustration with the process.
  • Nancy offered to help collabgoceo@gmail.com get the app developed through the company she is associated with, provided collabgoceo@gmail.com takes on the necessary work.


Market Research and Target Identification

  • Nancy emphasized the importance of conducting thorough market research to identify the right target audience for the app.
  • She highlighted the pitfalls of targeting too broad a market and not addressing real pain points.
  • Nancy advised starting with a hypothesis and identifying the first ten people who would be most eager to use the app.
  • She stressed the need to focus marketing and awareness campaigns on this smaller, more urgent target group.

He was incarcerated for 10 years for armed robbery, and near the end of his sentence, was beated by a guard

John seeks to build an AI software solution to serve the federal prison system and help moving people back the workforce, plan reentry etc

He is suing the prisons, but will drop the suit if they adopt his product

He has made a business plan, but lacks funding

John states that he is partnering with Google and has developer status with Google and Meta

He does not have a working prototype and has no clear customers other then bureau of prisons, who is unaware of his product at this time

He was not interested in any other help other than funding


Sent follow up email reaffirming my recommendations to product a working prototype

]Meeting Follow Up

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Carl and Marie met me at the Morey Tech Center to continue work on the executive summary. Marie has been working on the summary in her free time on a Google Doc shared with me. I reviewed it prior to their coming in, and it was taking good shape.

During our meeting, we projected the executive summary in the conference room and read through together. We re-phrased some of the verbiage as needed, and made sure that everything had a good flow. We also reformatted the executive summary to the same font, spacing, etc.

Marie brought up the proposal, use of funds, and scope of work pieces. We looked at the requested items that they would like to buy with grant dollars, and I showed her how to put these into her scope of work. She advised that she may need some help with the upload of documents, as her capabilities with her laptop were limited.

He's had more clients, there is some money in the bank. It feels like it's rolling out. His billboard goes up on June 9th. We speak again June 11th.


He is learning that his staff person may have some issues. She had a panic attack at the airport and has had some other problems. His entire business is riding on this person's ability to perform. I think it's a good idea to think about finding a second person as a hedge.


We talked about how AI can help. He was blown away

Good Afternoon!

 

I wanted to let you know that the grant opportunity in partnership with 20Fathoms is currently open. Key details are as follows:

 

  • Eligibility: Innovative startups and small businesses headquartered in Northwest Michigan.
  • Award Amount: Approximately $4,500 per business, totaling $75,000 across all recipients.
  • Selection Criteria: Based on the company's strategic need for funds and the expected impact on the local economy.
  • Grant Usage: Direct financial support for specific purchases of assets that will have a meaningful impact on business growth. Excludes building renovations/repairs or general expenses like marketing.
  • Application Period: May 16-25.

More information can be found

Company overview – Madala is shutting down his previous startup and forming a new Delaware C Corp (team of 3–4 contractors).

Strategic goal – seeking 5 pilot LOIs by summer with major hospitals (DMC, Johns Hopkins, UIC).

Healthcare system advice – pitch short 4-6 weeks, non-disruptive pilots with clear ROI and careful problem framing.

Funding discussion – BAF funds available via TechTown for legal/formation costs.

Next steps – Email Josh at TechTown (cc Shruti) for BAF; schedule strategy call with Sukhi, Shruti, and Madala; finalize formation with startup attorney.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Harry and I met with Christie and her lender, Kayleen Byrne of ChoiceOne Bank. We reviewed Christie’s financial projections and answered a few of the remaining questions that Kayleen had. We changed Christie’s client projections from 12 to 10, as during the last two years the facility operated at less than maximum capacity. If required, Harry and I will do a write-up of Christie’s expenses to explain how she arrived at each of the numbers. Kayleen let us know that an appraisal has been ordered for the facility – once we get the appraisal cost, we will add it along with the SBA fee and title company fee to the start up costs. She also reviewed the loan terms we had entered in and let us know to change the interest rate from 8% to 8.75%. Christie has been working with an attor

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Grant is working on refining his pitch deck for his space sensor startup but is facing the challenge of condensing complex technical and market information into a clear, compelling story for investors and customers. He’s trying to balance credibility with clarity—showcasing his background and technical depth without overwhelming the audience. He also needs to articulate the customer problem more clearly, differentiate his product from competitors, and define a realistic go-to-market and business model strategy, all while making the deck visually engaging and investor-ready.


I advised Grant to simplify and focus the pitch for in-person delivery first, aiming for a 5–8 minute version that clearly communicates the customer problem, solution, and why now. We walked through each section of the deck, restructuring the market slide using a three-circle model (beachhead, expansion, and total market), revising the business model to focus on sensor sales and software subscrip

Hi Charles,

Thank you again for meeting with me a little over a week ago — it was great hearing about your vision for U.S. Cleaning and Concierge. I appreciate your drive and clear ideas for both the cleaning and concierge sides of your business.

Apologies for the delay in getting back to you — I’ve been catching up on client work after being away for our MI SBDC state conference.

As we discussed, you’re off to a strong start with much of your equipment already in place. Next steps would be registering your LLC, getting your EIN, and continuing to develop your business plan so you have a solid roadmap moving forward. TRose Malone <trose.businesscoach@gmail.com>

Research the Vertical IQ valuation information. Email same to Jeff. Rerun the Internal Business Valuation for this company based on a stock sale. Small increase in value to $1,260,000 based on a stock sale. email Internal Business Valuation to Jeff with explanation.

Jeff,

 

Here is the Internal Business Valuation estimate of value if the company is sold as a stock sale. Not a big change in estimate: $1,260,000.

 

The program basically takes the Asset Sale value of $1,200,000 based on a multiple of SDE, adds the book value of the current assets and subtracts the book value of the current and long-term liabilities and adds the difference to the $1,200,000.  

 

Dan

  1. An asset sale rather than a stock sale.

Mariah Goos

Avril has missed a couple of appointments due to issues connecting to ZOOM. So we met via a phone call. She has recently relocated to Ironwood from Arkansas to take care of a parent.


Avril is looking for $5000 to start an Esthetician Salon (a skincare specialist who provides a variety of treatments to improve skin health and appearance) in Ironwood. She has picked out a location that she can rent at $675/month.

She currently has no financial resources, a credit score of around 580, and no collateral. She has created a basic business plan. I will follow up with her to forward this for me to take a look at. She also has no bank in the Ironwood area.

The main positive is that she is not seeking a large amount ($5,000).


At this point, it may be very difficult for her to get a bank loan of any kind with a low credit score and no collateral. SBA Microloan (?). I gently told her this. I also advised her to find a bank that she can work with.

Summary

Hi Julie, 

Funny - I was thinking about you today. 


The last 18 months have been very difficult. From trying to properly navigate a failing CrossFit business, to being forced to sell the building I bought as part of the original plan you helped me with back in 2019 in addition to dealing with the associated debt as a result of COVID. 


Needless to say, I've learned a ton trying to solve all these problems at the same time.

So sorry, I didn't get the message from you til that class was already over. My phone messages come through on their own time, and I was working with a client on a complicated set of financials for a business plan most of Tuesday. 


Were you able to attend any of the in person classes yet? There is just one more, the marketing one during Cheeseburger. I did get a waiver for you on that one, but they really are urging the in person attendance because of the interaction with other businesses. I will gladly fight for you, being sick is definitely a great reason to miss that. 


We do have a class from SBDC that you can take online to replace the financials if you still need to take it. You can register for our online learning system access it here:

Linda shared much of the same information she shared with Malia. She is seeking a grant to pay for an IP Attorney. The meeting became emotional and she shared that she is trying to dig herself out of poverty. Information shared during the meeting:

  1. She shared she is 68 years old, owns her home and property but has not running water or electricity. She wants to sell her Indian headdresses you hang from your windshield and dig herself out of poverty. She is very protective of her designs and believes she needs a patent or trademark on her designs to protect them.
  2. We discussed this and that IP Attorneys are not cheap. She was hoping she could get a grant to pay for one for her. We discussed the myths and facts about grants and that they take time to find and apply for if one is found.
  3. She shared that she was hit by a drunk driver when she was a truck driver in the state of South Dakota. She stated the person who hit her was friends with the cop and
closing at this time. no response after previous outreach

I met with Korynne and Mitch at the shop to discuss how their GLBBH grant application was going so far. Their executive summary was trimmed down, and I gave a final review. They have a great authority in their industry, and the summary was very well written. They were open to feedback, and felt good about the cuts they made to meet the 5-page limit.

We also discussed questions they had regarding the proposal form and use of funds documents. So far, all information that they had entered was spot-on, so I felt confident that they were on the right track. Afterwards, Mitch gave me a tour of the facility. I got to see the float pods and infared sauna rooms. It will be interesting to see how a salt room will affect traffic, as well as add to the established amenities.

Heather came in for assistance with the SBSH grant

We were able to get Brent Gettel on video to help us work though some questions during part of the meeting

Heather will continue to work on the application and send it to us for review
dorri dodson

Reviewed clients business plan with her. Helped her think through some scenarios to determine if she could get this business started and what a plan should look like to evaluate.

She wants to do an online business selling weight loss drugs. Her experience is in health care compliance and she has the medical background to understand that.

Needs to work through the start up costs and potential revenues and COGS. Fixed will be easier to predict.

Will work with her as she completes some of these tasks.

The client is currently a truck driver and is interested in transitioning to an owner-operator by acquiring his own tractor-trailer. During our meeting, we discussed the following key topics:


  • Startup and ongoing monthly expenses
  • Identifying ideal clients and strategies for securing consistent transportation contracts to maintain steady cash flow
  • Lead generation services

Client is planning to pivot to an Amazon store selling weighted blankets with covers. Wants to start with this popular item and then add additional corrdinating products for the bed and bedroom.

She is working through a 10 month paid mentorship from Emire Accelerator out of Dubai and they are helping with the Amazon intergration and analytics. She is still trying to refine and place the initial order. She has approx. %7,000 to spend on this business pivot. She has 2 you children and can't spend time designing and being out of the house at this time.

Met with Levi last week to discuss progress on his comic book and manga-focused retail business. He has officially formed his LLC and obtained his EIN. While he did not use the SBDC business plan template, he created his own version with basic financial projections. He completed a test run at Kalamazoo Comic Con, distributing 30 books and 50 business cards, generating $450 in sales. He has also explored interest at the Kalamazoo Flea Market.

Levi is working to identify a path to achieve $4K/month in gross profit but currently doubts the business can sustain him personally. He is considering a mixed-use property where he can live, operate his store, and potentially rent out other units. He currently markets via Instagram reels and has distributor relationships with Ingraham and Southern Hobby.

Levi sees three paths forward: accepting the business may not be financially sustainable, finding very affordable rent, or waiting things out. He e

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Response to Karmel, regarding her questions about the GLBBH Business Grant:


Hello,


I’ve just spent a total of 3 hours on this today, it may not look like it….


I do want to keep the green part at 25-35% given the new information of Rockford apartments washer and dryer price increase. Also it makes sense if we do get the grant and advertising dollars with the smaller washers we would see an increase in revenue with the smaller washer and having more machines anyway.


I’m unsure how to answer the increase in revenue from a radio advertising perspective. Angie had not gotten back to me. My research shows significantly more than what you say you think we would see a return on. I get that from just an add viewpoint but I’m considering a targeted digital to phone regarding full service viewpoint. We could say that same amount over a 12 month period?


I’ve highlighted or bold MY main areas of concerns… I get

Daniel joined our call to go over the live plan. The numbers looked pretty good and we felt confident after having a former banker check them out that we are on the right track. Tara is going to bring it to State Savings Bank in the next couple weeks and Northern Initiatives. We will meet again in two weeks to see what else she needs assistance with.

Ben is in business and looking for financing. This was just a follow up email.



Ben Richey
Nicole Young Potvin

Sent: Thursday, May 22, 2025 3:20 PM

To: Sue DeVries (MEDC) <devriess1@michigan.org>

Cc: Marie Elliott <elliotm1@gvsu.edu>

I can reschedule, hopefully the calendar's not too far out.


I do have a question, I was looking at grants and found one from a group called the Freed Fellowship. Are your or anyone in your office familiar with it?

Becky sent me a new copy of her GLBBH executive summary to review prior to our meeting. I reviewed it, and all sounds good - she added some new information about the use of funds, updated with the new quotes and asks that she and Chris considered over the last couple of weeks.

We met at 3:30 to do one final look at the summary, and then I helped Becky polish her supplemental documents. She was panicked about filling out the table on both the proposal and use of funds document. Both asked for the same information, but in a different order, meaning that one couldn't just copy the table. We worked out a way to list the information quickly on both. We also added her last quote to the Scope of Work document.

From there, we did a final read-through again, and Becky wanted me to watch as she submitted each document. We confirmed the review process, and the award notice deadline of June 13th. We will be in touch shortly to debrief and discuss any new questions as needed. <

Stephanie has elected to be s-corp. Accountant Ryan Markham wants her to use QB and start payroll. Assisted her with starting QB. Wave transactinos through April 2025, then starting with QB with May 1. Helped her get bank accounts connected. Still need to do credit card and work on payroll setup. She needs to find out from accountant if she has a business tax account setup for employee withholding.

Virtual Meeting / Review of Draft Business Plan


Brenda provided a draft business plan based on our previous conversation. Reviewed business plan and made commentary regarding sourcing of product and finding local sources of information regarding the summer/vacation traffic that will be key to the business. Referred her to one of the business planning webinars to help fill in some parts of the plan and mentioned we would do LivePlan as things began to solidify so input into the system would be readily available. She will be meeting with an area canoe livery and discuss summer traffic, etc. Indicated she will likely have to create an LLC to own the building but indicated an attorney should be consulted for formal legal guidance if the opportunity progresses. Shes done research on utilities and vendors. Consultant to provide information on general grant information, market data (Verticle IQ) preparing a use of proceeds based on a sales agreement.

ClientIntake.CompanyName: KZ Magner Co DBA Krazy Jim's Blimpyburger

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Reviewed Business Valuation Dan sent over. Spoke with Emily about value. Emily is now 25% owner of the company. 2 of her 3 brothers work in the company but are not communicating about what they want to do moving forward. EMily is working with a potential buy as well as settling the estate and managing the bank end of the business. She wants out.


NS - We have a meeting scheduled tomorrow with the whole family. I will lay out all possible plans and try to get feedback from the family.


Basically there is an SBA loan that the brothers can not financially assume, therefore they need to come up with $130K to pay it off or sell the business.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM: Marketing and sales

 

RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN: We Zoomed to update her case. She has now won about $20000 in various grant pitches and expects a few more. This included another Start Garden, one in Muskegon, and MDARD, too. She tracks down various sponsors and signs up for their newsletters. She had a SWOT done for her business by the Start Garden, and we reviewed their comments. She noted that sponsors like to hear about one's planned use of funds and how they would be spent to grow the business.

 

NEXT STEPS & FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS: She will send me her SWOT analysis.

Aaron is working on two ventures—a warehouse management app called Warehouse Pro, and a consultancy agency offering websites and AI tools. He’s struggling with early-stage challenges, including a lack of customer validation for the app, how to differentiate from existing WMS competitors, and managing a 50-50 partnership where contributions may become uneven. He's also working to refine his pitch for an upcoming competition and identify a clearer target market, as his current approach of serving all warehouses is too broad. Aaron is trying to identify warehouse clients with specific needs and limited IT infrastructure, but needs more direction on customer discovery and effective pitching.


I recommended that Aaron refine his customer discovery efforts by using NAICS codes and beginning conversations with approachable staff. I shared a customer outreach email template and suggested framing interviews as learning conversations rather than sales calls. I also encoura

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Summary

Introductions and Backgrounds

  • ptoenjes@curacollaborative.com introduced Nancy and Rob to each other.
  • Nancy shared that they have a strong tech background with a history of entrepreneurship, having been involved in 14 startups with 8 exits.
  • Nancy mentioned their experience spinning technology out of the MIT Media Lab and selling it to Microsoft, as well as running a mobile cloud service deployed as a white label to every North American carrier.

Brenae has lost some steam where building a business plan is concerned. While talking, she stated that she hasn't allowed herself to think about where the business could go. She also wasn't sure what she should charge or what the 3-year period of the business plan might bring her.


She knows she would like to work for herself and that she could see a building with a white wall that has her logo on it.


We talked about pricing as a replacement for the salary she would need to make and learned that $75/hour at 20 hours per week wouldn't be unreasonable to work and make up for a salary and benefits.


Focus Four came into the conversation for the Whirlwind and vision chapters that I think could help her figure out what she wants to build and find time to do it.

ClientIntake.BusinessType: Real Estate and Rental and Leasing

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Has the LLC but needs to register the EIN. We worked through the deep 36-month projections and discussed cash-flow vs profit. Wants to avoid getting a loan and is no longer seeking investors. Likely will have to take out a mortgage on the purchase of the land and containers. Client feels they have a better understanding of what will be required financially and how to look at the numbers going forward. Client is still looking at local land options and is considering options near Duck Lake (due North of Albion). Lots of 2nd homes/cabins and they are very close together on the lake. Retirees and more disposable income there. Client prefers to meet in person - please advise when I'll be in the area.

Next steps - client will also speak with their accountant regarding the tax implications - owner's draw, 1099's, etc. that will impact their taxable income. This will move slowly and perhaps something will be in place by year-end. Stay in touch and reach out monthly.

James

Emily,

Here it the Executive form completed except for any touch-ups for tomorrow morning meeting.

Hi Tom,


Just wanted to say REALLY great work on the executive summary! 

You hit all of the key areas that you needed to, and my feedback is mainly on formatting. I made some adjustments to make the font uniform, and I added the headings back in to help break it up. I also highlighted some suggestions to change phrasing slightly. Looking forward to talking more in person shortly!


Best,

Emily

Summary: The client sent her NSF project pitch for another review.


Action: I have taken a look at their pitch again and I think it looks really, really good. I have a couple of pretty minor suggestions and then I think it is ready to submit. Technology Innovation: I believe that the first and second paragraph can be combined. Chat GPT has a habit of separating into smaller paragraphs and 1 sentence paragraphs tend to be an indicator. So I would try to avoid that.  The second paragraph is the only paragraph in this section which is in first person. I would either add more first person into this section or I would start this paragraph with something like “The proposed technology addresses the challenge….” I think everyone knows what HIPAA, but maybe not BAA and PHI so they might want to spell those out (last paragraph). In general after this point, don’t respell out acronyms like WGS, HPO, etc. That is just a waste of characters and repetitive. This is for all the fu
